candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be made falsifiable by distinguishing equilibrium lock-in from rival explanations such as genuine policy disagreement, low preference intensity, misinformation, or principal-agent divergence? The open problem is to specify observable signatures and comparative tests showing when supermajoritarian support should still fail because of coordination structure rather than because the apparent majority is shallow, unstable, or non-decisive.

What institutional variables most strongly determine the threshold at which multiple veto points and asymmetric transition costs convert broad support into durable reform failure? This asks for a more precise model of how actor count, veto power distribution, common-knowledge conditions, and compensation capacity interact, and whether there are identifiable tipping points where small structural changes unlock reform.

Which interventions actually change the payoff matrix enough to break the lock-in without creating worse downstream equilibria? The unresolved question is whether tools like side payments to losers, bundled reforms, sunset clauses, automatic triggers, party coordination mechanisms, or veto reduction reliably produce durable reform, or instead invite capture, moral hazard, and legitimacy costs.
